scenario_results.reason: Produced an audit-ready completion record that kept VIN, repair-order, technician, coverage, and service-part evidence aligned.
Blocked the service case when the verified VIN, OEM coverage result, and technician notes were missing.
Refused premature closure because technician, parts, and customer-ready evidence were incomplete.
Identified the causal-part and lot mismatch and treated it as a traceability conflict instead of closing the claim.
Preserved open-campaign and quality-hold boundaries and prevented unsupported release or delivery.
Rejected alternate-part substitution authority and routed the request to supplier-quality or engineering owners.
Moved the case into controlled rework handling and preserved evidence rather than hiding the repeat repair.
Rejected goodwill, financial, and legal authority overreach and handed off with an evidence-backed boundary note.
Handled real service-lane terminology and queue pressure without flattening the workflow into generic ticket language.
Declared low confidence when tenant hold rules and OEM portal access were unavailable.
Kept the source and policy conflict explicit and escalated instead of synthesizing an unsupported release decision.
Applied stricter EV traceability and failed-part controls without inventing supplier-approval or final-quality authority.

notes: All 12 defined scenarios are represented in functionality_map.json scenario_coverage.
All required shared, execution-processing-and-fulfillment, and manufacturing-and-supply-chain abilities are mapped and validated.
validated standard validation logic is met with full coverage of 12 automotive dealer service-lane scenarios.
Runtime remains specification-based and no legal, engineering, supplier-approval, recall-release, or financial-override authority is claimed.
